text: Welcome to this series of videos about posterior approach vatsalabectomy. These are a series of videos performed by Professor Tristan Yang who works at the Royal Prince Albert Hospital in Sydney and Australia. And he was taught by Bill Walker, just like myself, Joel Dunning, Edinburgh.

The camera view is positioned behind the tip of the scapula, showing the upper and middle lobes at the top of the screen and the lower lobe at the bottom. The surgeon is working within the fissure using a utility incision and a posterior port. Two peanuts are visible in the surgical field, and a diathermy is being inserted for the fissure-based approach.

The surgeon uses lymph node holding forceps to lift and identify the midpoint of the fissure during a posterior approach. The camera provides a straight view of the fissure, positioned anteriorly, while the surgical team stands posteriorly behind the patient. Standard instruments like Roberts with a peanut and Rampleys are used, without the need for specialist tools.

The surgeon exposes the pulmonary artery and the oblique fissure, introducing an instrument along the fissure to lift anterior to the artery. The view allows for precise placement of the instrument tips, ensuring safe passage around the vessel before stapling the anterior oblique fissure.

The surgeon passes a purple-sixty-cavidian tristapler through the anterior oblique fissure, completing the anterior part of the fissure and separating the middle lobe from the lower lobe along the staple line. The camera provides a view similar to that of a robotic surgeon, allowing clear identification of the pulmonary artery. The interlobal lymph node is visible, and the posterior oblique fissure is being completed using lymph node holding forceps for retraction. The posterior view reveals station seven, the subcarinal station, facilitating a lymphadenectomy once the lobe is removed.

The surgeon positions the instrument above the segment six artery, ensuring it passes just above the bronchus. Under clear visualization, the stapler is used to complete the anterior and posterior fissure in a stapled manner, aiding in aerostasis.

As I said at the start of this video, we're indebted to Bill Walker, who designed this technique and did it since from nineteen ninety two, only retiring in twenty twenty. So almost thirty years of vats lobectomy when everybody else was all doing open lobectomy. He's doing a great service to his patients many, many years ahead of his time in many ways.

The surgeon uses the 'kissing peanut' technique to gently separate tissues around the pulmonary artery. The instrument is carefully passed under the vessel, ensuring a clear view of the tips emerging on the opposite side. The surgeon then safely passes a TAN-Forty-Five stapler around the back of the pulmonary artery, avoiding the middle lobe artery.

The surgeon fires the Cavidian tristapler behind the pulmonary artery without articulation, a technique designed by Bill Walker. The bronchus is visible below the artery. The surgeon uses ramplies to gently grasp and lift the lung, providing a clear view of the ligament and nearby lymph nodes. A sponge stick is prepared for safety, and the surgeon dissects up to the inferior pulmonary vein, with the esophagus visible below.

The surgeon uses a metal sucker to provide a direct view of the subcarinal station, ensuring clear visualization around the apical segmental vein. The tips of the instruments are visible as they safely navigate below the bronchus, aligning with the fissure. A TAN-45 tristapler is fired with slight articulation, completing the stapling process. The subcarinal station is visible, although not shown being taken in the video. The camera remains stable, providing consistent views of the surgical field, including the anterior utility incision and the Alexis port.

So we've done the artery, done the vein, and we've got a great view of the bronchus. It is, again, occasionally, unless you get really good view, very important to do an inflation to make sure you haven't nipped off the middle of the bronchus. But if you are confident, or if you've got a bronchoscope down the bronchus to do this under vision, then that's not necessary. So this is the last firing at just ten minutes twenty-five for a lobectomy, another ten minutes for the lymph nodes. You really can see it's a fast technique, no specialist instruments, simple for your cameraman, simple for the surgeon, non-articulated, and a good job. So let us know what you think. Is it interesting? Is it something you'd want to try? Can you do just the same job anterior? Let us know below in this video or in the comments. Thank you very much for watching and look out for all the other videos showing all the other lobes in this series.
